headline: Echoblast Q1 FY2030 Earnings Call Transcript
rendered_text:
# Echoblast, Inc. Q1 FY2031 Earnings Call Transcript
**Call Date:** May 15, 2030 | **For Quarter Ended:** March 31, 2030

---

**Operator:** Good afternoon, and welcome to Echoblast's Q1 2031 earnings call. My name is Sarah, and I'll be your operator today. At this time, all participants are in a listen-only mode. After the presentation, we will conduct a question-and-answer session. Please note that this call is being recorded, and a replay will be available on Echoblast's investor relations website. Before we begin, I'd like to remind everyone that certain statements made during this call may constitute forward-looking statements within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act. These statements are subject to risks and uncertainties, and actual results may differ materially. For a detailed discussion of these risks, please refer to Echoblast's most recent SEC filings. I'll now turn the call over to Youssef Wilson, Echoblast's Head of Investor Relations.

---

**Youssef Wilson:** Thanks, Sarah. Good afternoon, everyone, and thank you for joining us for Echoblast's Q1 2031 earnings call. We released our financial results this morning, and a copy of the press release and supplemental financial data are available on our investor relations website at ir.echoblast.ai. I'm pleased to introduce Will Coleman, our Chief Executive Officer and founder, followed by Luis Tran, our Chief Financial Officer. Will, over to you.

---

**Will Coleman:** Thanks, Youssef, and good afternoon, everyone. It's great to be here for our first earnings call as a public company. I want to start by saying how proud I am of what the Echoblast team has accomplished in the first quarter of 2031, and I'm excited to share our progress with you.

Let me begin with the headline numbers. We delivered $33.4 million in total revenue for Q1, representing 18% year-over-year growth and 4% sequential growth from Q4. Our annualized recurring revenue, or ARR, reached $401.2 million, up 19% year-over-year. This is a significant milestone for us—when we founded Echoblast in January 2025, we had essentially zero revenue. By the end of 2025, we'd hit approximately $450 thousand in annualized revenue. By the end of 2026, we were at roughly $6 million ARR. By 2027, $24 million. By 2028, $78 million. And now, here we are in 2031 with $401 million. That's a journey we're very proud of.

Our gross margin expanded to 24.6% in the quarter, up from 23.1% in Q4, reflecting the operating leverage we're achieving as we scale our infrastructure and optimize our utilization rates. We're also pleased to report that our operating margin reached 7.0% on a non-GAAP basis, demonstrating that we're balancing growth investments with profitability.

Now, let me talk about our product portfolio and how our customers are using Echoblast to power their AI workloads. We operate three main product families: Echoblast Cloud, our on-demand GPU-rental product; Echoblast Train, our managed training and fine-tuning platform; and Echoblast Inference, our inference-optimization service. In Q1, Cloud revenue was $18.1 million, Train revenue was $6.3 million, and Inference revenue was $4.9 million. The diversification across these products is exactly what we intended, and it's giving us multiple vectors for growth.

On the customer front, we're seeing strong traction with some of the most innovative companies in AI. We're honored to count Harvey, Cursor, and Civitai among our publicly-named customers. These are companies that are pushing the boundaries of what's possible with large language models and generative AI, and they trust Echoblast to power their compute infrastructure. The fact that they've chosen us tells us we're delivering real value.

From a fleet perspective, we now own and operate 46,353 GPUs across multiple regions and vendors. This represents a deliberate diversification strategy. We learned important lessons from industry disruptions, and we've made sure that our capacity is spread across different geographies and hardware providers to give our customers the reliability and redundancy they need.

I also want to highlight a strategic initiative that's become increasingly important to our business: Echoblast Marketplace. This is our third-party capacity aggregation product, and it's allowing us to extend our platform beyond just our owned hardware. By bringing in capacity from other providers, we're able to offer our customers a unified interface to access a broader pool of compute resources. This is a meaningful lever for growth that doesn't require us to own and operate every single GPU.

Looking ahead, we remain focused on three strategic priorities. First, we're deepening our relationships with existing customers and expanding their usage. Second, we're expanding our geographic footprint and adding new regions to serve customers with lower-latency, lower-cost options. Third, we're investing in our product roadmap to make training and inference even faster and more efficient for our customers.

We're confident in our trajectory, and we believe the market for AI compute infrastructure is in its infancy. The demand we're seeing is real, and we're well-positioned to capture a meaningful share of this opportunity.

With that, let me turn things over to Luis for the financial details.

---

**Luis Tran:** Thanks, Will, and good afternoon. I'll walk through our financial results in more detail and provide some forward guidance.

**Revenue and Segment Performance**

In Q1, we achieved total revenue of $33.438 million, up 18% year-over-year from $28.3 million in Q1 2030. Breaking this down by segment:

- **Echoblast Cloud** generated $18.143 million in revenue, representing 54% of total revenue. This segment grew 16% year-over-year and reflects strong demand for on-demand GPU capacity from AI development teams.
- **Echoblast Train** generated $6.349 million in revenue, representing 19% of total revenue. This segment grew 22% year-over-year, indicating that our managed training platform is resonating with customers who want to outsource the complexity of distributed training orchestration.
- **Echoblast Inference** generated $4.929 million in revenue, representing 15% of total revenue. This segment grew 24% year-over-year, the fastest-growing segment, as customers increasingly move models into production and optimize for inference cost and latency.
- The remaining $3.977 million, or 12%, came from our Marketplace and other services, which grew 15% year-over-year.

**Profitability Metrics**

Gross margin for Q1 was 24.6%, up 150 basis points sequentially from 23.1% in Q4. This improvement was driven by three factors: first, higher utilization rates on our owned fleet; second, improved pricing power as we've expanded our product offerings; and third, operational efficiencies in our data center operations. We expect gross margin to continue to expand as we scale, with a target of 30-32% by the end of 2031.

Operating margin on a non-GAAP basis was 7.0% in Q1, up from 5.2% in Q4. This reflects operating leverage in our R&D and sales and marketing functions as revenue scales faster than headcount growth. Our current headcount is 287 employees, up from 268 in Q4, representing disciplined hiring.

**Cash Flow and Capital Allocation**

Free cash flow in Q1 was positive $2.1 million, reflecting the strong unit economics of our business. We generated approximately $4.3 million in operating cash flow, and our capital expenditures were $2.2 million, primarily for GPU acquisitions and data center infrastructure. We expect capex as a percentage of revenue to remain in the 6-8% range as we maintain our fleet and add capacity to support growth.

**Balance Sheet**

We ended Q1 with $187 million in cash and equivalents, down from $195 million at the end of Q4, reflecting our capex investments and working capital needs. We have no debt outstanding. We believe our balance sheet provides us with significant flexibility to invest in growth opportunities while maintaining financial discipline.

**Forward Guidance**

For Q2 2031, we expect total revenue in the range of $35 million to $36 million, representing sequential growth of 5% to 8%. This assumes continued strength in Cloud and Train, offset by typical seasonal patterns in Inference. We expect non-GAAP operating margin to be in the range of 6.5% to 7.5%.

For the full year 2031, we expect total revenue in the range of $145 million to $150 million, representing year-over-year growth of 16% to 20%. We expect non-GAAP operating margin to expand to 8% to 10% as we achieve greater scale and operating leverage.

These guidance ranges reflect our current visibility into customer demand and our confidence in our ability to execute. However, as with any forward-looking statements, there are risks and uncertainties that could affect our results.

**Non-GAAP Reconciliation**

For transparency, our non-GAAP operating margin excludes stock-based compensation expense of $1.8 million in Q1 and depreciation and amortization of $0.9 million. A full reconciliation of GAAP to non-GAAP metrics is available in our supplemental financial data on our IR website.

With that, I'll turn it back to Youssef to moderate our Q&A session.

---

**Youssef Wilson:** Thanks, Luis. We have time for several questions today. I'd like to remind everyone to please keep to one question and one brief follow-up. Let's start with Walter Park from Morgan Stanley.

---

**Walter Park (Morgan Stanley):** Thanks, Youssef. Will, Luis, congrats on the strong quarter. My question is on capacity utilization. You mentioned 46,353 GPUs owned and operated. Can you give us a sense of what your average utilization rate is across the fleet, and how that's trending? I'm trying to understand if there's room for pricing power as you move toward full utilization.

**Will Coleman:** Great question, Walter. We don't disclose our specific utilization rate as a point metric, but I can tell you directionally that we're seeing utilization improve sequentially. The way I think about it is that our gross margin expansion from 23.1% to 24.6% is a pretty good proxy for what's happening on the utilization side. As our customers scale their workloads and as we optimize our scheduling algorithms, we're getting more revenue per GPU per hour. That said, we still have meaningful room to grow before we hit a point where capacity becomes a constraint. We're actively adding capacity in key regions, and we're using our Marketplace product to fill gaps when our owned capacity is tight. So we feel good about our ability to serve demand without compromising our margins.

**Walter Park (Morgan Stanley):** Follow-up: on the Marketplace product, can you help us understand how that's scaling? Is it a meaningful revenue contributor today, or is it more of a strategic hedge?

**Luis Tran:** It's a bit of both, Walter. In Q1, Marketplace and other services contributed $3.977 million, which is about 12% of total revenue. That's growing 15% year-over-year. It's not yet our largest segment, but it's becoming increasingly strategic. The way we think about it is that Marketplace allows us to offer our customers a unified interface to access compute from multiple providers, which increases our stickiness and our wallet share. It also allows us to serve demand that exceeds our owned capacity without having to own and operate every single GPU ourselves. So while it's not our primary focus today, we see it as a meaningful growth vector over time.

---

**Claudia Murphy (Goldman Sachs):** Thanks, Youssef. Luis, I want to dig into the gross margin trajectory you outlined. You said you expect 30-32% by end of 2031. That's a meaningful jump from 24.6% today. Can you walk us through the key drivers? Is it utilization, pricing, mix shift, or a combination?

**Luis Tran:** Great question, Claudia. It's definitely a combination. Let me break it down. First, utilization: we expect to drive meaningful utilization gains as we continue to optimize our scheduling and as our customer base scales. Second, pricing: we've been disciplined about not racing to the bottom on price. As our product gets better and our customer satisfaction scores improve, we're seeing less price sensitivity. Third, mix shift: our Train and Inference segments have higher gross margins than Cloud, and those are growing faster than Cloud. So as the mix shifts, that naturally pulls up the blended margin. Fourth, infrastructure costs: as we scale, we're getting better pricing from our hardware vendors and our data center operators. So all of those factors combined, we're targeting 30-32% by end of year. That's our base case, but if we execute well on utilization and pricing, we could exceed that.

**Claudia Murphy (Goldman Sachs):** Follow-up: and what's the implied capex to get there? Are you planning to add significantly more GPUs, or are you going to be more measured?

**Luis Tran:** We're planning to be measured, Claudia. Our current capex as a percentage of revenue is in the 6-8% range, and we expect that to remain stable or even decline slightly as we mature. So on a $145-150 million revenue base for 2031, that implies capex of roughly $9-12 million for the year. We're not planning a major expansion of our fleet. We're focused on optimizing the fleet we have and using Marketplace to fill gaps. So I'd expect capex to be a headwind on free cash flow, but not a major one.

---

**Samir Watkins (SemiAnalysis):** Thanks. Will, I want to ask about competitive dynamics. We've seen some of the hyperscalers—AWS, Google, Azure—get more aggressive on GPU rental pricing. We're also seeing some startups in the space. How are you thinking about competition, and what's your defensibility? What keeps customers from going to AWS?

**Will Coleman:** Samir, that's the right question to ask. Look, competition is real. The hyperscalers have enormous scale and distribution advantages. But we think we have some defensibility. First, we're purpose-built for AI. We're not a general-purpose cloud provider trying to bolt on GPU rental. Everything we do is optimized for training and inference workloads. Second, we're faster to iterate. We can add a new GPU type or a new region in weeks, not quarters. Third, we have a better developer experience. Our customers tell us that our APIs are cleaner, our documentation is better, and our support is more responsive than the alternatives. Fourth, we're pricing aggressively but not suicidally. We're not trying to undercut AWS on price; we're trying to offer better value. And fifth, we have Echoblast Train and Echoblast Inference, which are differentiated products that the hyperscalers don't have at the same level of maturity. So we're not naïve about competition, but we think we have a sustainable moat. And frankly, the fact that we're seeing strong customer adoption from companies like Harvey, Cursor, and Civitai tells us that our value proposition is resonating.

**Samir Watkins (SemiAnalysis):** Follow-up: on the Train and Inference products specifically, can you help us understand the competitive landscape there? Are you competing with open-source frameworks, or are you competing with other commercial players?

**Will Coleman:** It's a mix. On Train, we're competing with open-source frameworks, but we're also competing with custom in-house solutions that large enterprises build. Our value prop is that we handle all the operational complexity—distributed training orchestration, fault tolerance, resource scheduling—so that our customers can focus on their models and their research. On Inference, we're competing with both open-source solutions like vLLM and commercial players who offer inference optimization. But we're differentiated because we're integrated with the Echoblast Cloud infrastructure, so we can offer end-to-end optimization from the model level all the way down to the hardware level. So I'd say we're competing on value, not just on price, and I think that's a sustainable position.

---

**Elena Wade (Bloomberg):** Thanks. Luis, I want to come back to the ARR number. You said $401.2 million. That's a pretty significant jump from where you were at IPO. Can you help us understand what drove that? Is it net new customers, expansion within existing customers, or pricing increases?

**Luis Tran:** Great question, Elena. It's primarily expansion within existing customers. We've been very focused on expanding our wallet share with our top accounts. Our largest customers are using us for multiple products—Cloud, Train, and Inference—and they're expanding their usage over time as they scale their AI workloads. We've also added new customers, but the growth is really driven by expansion. I'd also note that our dollar-based net retention rate, which we'll start disclosing regularly, was very healthy in Q1, though we're not going to disclose the specific number today. But the fact that our ARR is growing faster than our revenue suggests that we have strong expansion dynamics and that our customers are sticky.

**Elena Wade (Bloomberg):** Follow-up: and on the customer acquisition front, how many customers do you have today, and what's the average contract value?

**Will Coleman:** We have approximately 850 customers today, up from 720 at the end of Q4. So we're adding customers, but our focus is really on expansion. Average contract value is something we're not going to disclose precisely, but I can tell you that it's healthy and growing. We have a mix of small self-service customers and large enterprise customers, and the enterprise customers are where we're seeing the most expansion. So we're building a very balanced customer base.

---

**Youssef Wilson:** We have time for one more question. Let's go to David Chen from Bernstein.

**David Chen (Bernstein):** Thanks. Will, I want to ask about the roadmap. What are you most excited about for the rest of 2031? What are the key product milestones we should be watching for?

**Will Coleman:** Great question, David. I'm excited about three things. First, we're launching a new region in Europe in Q2, which is going to unlock a whole new set of customers and reduce latency for our European customers. Second, we're shipping a major upgrade to Echoblast Train that's going to make it significantly faster and more cost-effective for large-scale training runs. We've done some internal benchmarking, and the improvements are really exciting. Third, we're expanding our Marketplace product to include CPU capacity, not just GPUs. This is going to allow us to serve a broader set of workloads and increase our addressable market. So I think 2031 is going to be a really strong year for product innovation, and I'm excited to share more details as we progress through the year.

---

**Youssef Wilson:** Thank you all for the great questions. Will, any closing remarks?

**Will Coleman:** Just a quick one. I want to thank the Echoblast team for an incredible Q1. We're executing well, our customers are happy, and we're building something really meaningful. I'm grateful to our investors for their support, and I'm excited about the road ahead. We'll be at the Goldman Sachs Technology Conference in a couple of weeks, and we'll also be hosting our first investor day in the fall. So stay tuned for more. Thanks for being on the call.

**Youssef Wilson:** Thanks, Will. This concludes Echoblast's Q1 2031 earnings call. A replay of this call will be available on our investor relations website within 24 hours. Thank you all for joining us, and have a great day.

---

**[END OF TRANSCRIPT]**
